response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend roughly a few times weekly on social media

response_parsed.opinion: I'll be honest, dealing with fake content on social media really stresses me out sometimes. I'm not on there constantly, just a few times a week to check in on family and see what's happening around Kansas City, but even that limited exposure shows me how much nonsense is floating around out there. It worries me because my daughter uses social media way more than I do, and I wonder what she's seeing and believing. At the salon, my clients talk about things they've read online, and sometimes I can tell it's probably not true, but I don't like confronting people about it because I hate conflict. I just try to listen and be understanding. What I've learned is that you really can't believe everything you see, even when it looks official or comes from someone you know. I try to pause before sharing anything, though I'll admit I'm not always great at double-checking things before I react. When something makes me anxious or upset, which happens more than I'd like, I've started asking myself if it seems too extreme or designed to make me feel a certain way. That usually helps me step back. I also think about whether the information matches what I see in my actual life. Like, if someone posts something wild about our city or about health stuff that affects my work, I compare it to what I actually experience day to day. My clients come from all walks of life, and their real stories are usually more complicated than what gets posted online. I wish there was an easier way to know what's real, but since there isn't, I just try to be careful and not let myself get too worked up over things I read. I've also started asking my daughter about things I see that worry me, and sometimes she helps me understand what's actually going on versus what's exaggerated. It's not a perfect system, and honestly, the whole thing makes me a bit nervous about where we're headed as a society, but taking breaks from social media and focusing on real conversations with real people in my chair at work helps keep me grounded. I think that's really the key, staying connected to actual reality and not letting the online world take over your peace of mind.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is a 55-year-old self-employed hairdresser with lower secondary education, anxious temperament, high agreeableness, low extraversion, and moderate neuroticism. She uses social media only a few times weekly. Her response should reflect: (1) anxiety about fake content and its effects, (2) concern for her daughter, (3) reluctance to confront others due to high agreeableness and low extraversion, (4) practical, grounded perspective from her work experience, (5) informal, conversational tone matching her education level and occupation, (6) admission of imperfection in managing the issue due to low conscientiousness, (7) reliance on real-world experience and personal relationships over systematic fact-checking. The tone is honest, somewhat worried, empathetic, and humble.
response_parsed.tone: conversational, honest, somewhat anxious, empathetic, humble, grounded in practical experience
